items.title: Azure SQL Database continuity posture
items.summary: Strong in-region platform HA is available, but regional DR only starts when cross-region features and client failover behavior are designed and tested together.
items.notes: Strong in-region platform HA is available, but regional DR only starts when cross-region features and client failover behavior are designed and tested together.
items.guardrail: Do not describe zone redundancy as regional DR.
recommendedActions: Validate tier and regional support for zone redundancy before setting the baseline.
Use failover groups when connection continuity matters across multiple databases.
Record acceptable replication lag before exporting leadership-ready conclusions.

references.label: Business continuity in Azure SQL Database

detail.haSummary: Azure SQL Database includes built-in platform HA. Zone redundancy raises resiliency to zonal failures within a region and Azure handles zone failover automatically.
detail.drSummary: For regional DR, use active geo-replication, failover groups, or geo-restore. Failover groups are generally the cleaner multi-database DR option because listener endpoints remain stable.
limitations: Zone redundancy is HA not regional DR; DR precision is feature and workload dependent
recommendedFor: Mission-critical SQL workloads needing managed HA and strong DR options
notRecommendedFor: Workloads that need guaranteed synchronous cross-region zero-loss semantics
testGuidance: Run failover drills, validate application retry behavior, and test geo-restore separately from failover groups

items.title: Azure App Service web tier posture
items.summary: App Service can provide stable in-region HA on supported Premium plans, but regional continuity still depends on a duplicated application stack and replicated dependencies.
items.notes: App Service can provide stable in-region HA on supported Premium plans, but regional continuity still depends on a duplicated application stack and replicated dependencies.
items.guardrail: App Service remains a single-region service even when zone redundant.
recommendedActions: Keep application state out of local App Service storage.
Duplicate certificates, secrets, and configuration in both regions.
Route failover through a deliberate global traffic layer.

references.label: Multi-region App Service app approaches for disaster recovery

detail.haSummary: App Service supports zone redundancy on supported Premium plans. The platform handles zone failure detection and traffic redistribution inside the region.
detail.drSummary: App Service is a single-region service. Regional DR requires duplicating the app in another region and combining it with traffic management and replicated dependencies.
limitations: Not all scale units support zone redundancy; overprovisioning is recommended if capacity must survive zone loss
recommendedFor: HTTP apps that can keep state outside the web tier and duplicate deployment across regions
notRecommendedFor: Apps that keep critical state only on local App Service storage
testGuidance: Test regional failover through the chosen traffic routing layer and verify deployment parity between regions

items.title: Azure Storage redundancy review posture
items.summary: Storage redundancy options are well documented, but the review must still separate local or zonal protection from true cross-region continuity.
items.notes: Storage redundancy options are well documented, but the review must still separate local or zonal protection from true cross-region continuity.
items.guardrail: LRS and ZRS are not regional DR strategies.
recommendedActions: Align redundancy choice with required read behavior in the secondary region.
Test planned failover procedures before calling the pattern leadership ready.
Disclose replication lag whenever GRS or GZRS is part of the export.

references.label: Azure storage disaster recovery planning and failover

detail.haSummary: Storage redundancy settings determine local and zonal resiliency. Microsoft recommends ZRS in the primary region for high availability and for Data Lake Storage workloads.
detail.drSummary: Regional DR comes from GRS or GZRS with failover, and optional read access comes from RA-GRS or RA-GZRS.
limitations: Azure Files does not support RA-GRS or RA-GZRS; secondary region access differs by redundancy option
recommendedFor: Storage-centric workloads where redundancy choice can be aligned to business RTO and RPO
notRecommendedFor: Teams assuming LRS or ZRS alone provide regional DR
testGuidance: Exercise planned failover where supported and validate client behavior against secondary endpoints

items.title: Azure Key Vault secret dependency posture
items.summary: Key Vault has strong built-in resiliency, but enterprise reviews still need to check application dependency behavior, regional access patterns, and break-glass design.
items.notes: Key Vault has strong built-in resiliency, but enterprise reviews still need to check application dependency behavior, regional access patterns, and break-glass design.
items.guardrail: Do not present built-in resiliency as a substitute for dependency testing.
recommendedActions: Validate application retry behavior against transient Key Vault failures.
Document secret access dependencies in the wider failover runbook.
Keep break-glass access procedures outside the open review surface.

detail.haSummary: Key Vault provides built-in intra-region redundancy and automatically enables zone redundancy in regions that support availability zones, with no extra configuration required.
detail.drSummary: Key Vault also replicates to a paired region in supported geographies and Microsoft may trigger failover on a best-effort basis, but failover can take hours and the secondary becomes read-only. For stronger DR objectives, use separate vaults in multiple regions with backup, restore, and application failover logic.
limitations: Microsoft-managed regional failover is best-effort, can take several hours, and results in read-only secondary behavior
recommendedFor: Applications that need highly available secret retrieval with moderate regional DR tolerance
notRecommendedFor: Workloads needing tightly bounded regional RTO and RPO from Microsoft-managed failover alone
testGuidance: Test secret retrieval during failover simulations, backup and restore procedures, and custom dual-vault failover logic

items.title: API Management tier posture
items.summary: API Management guidance is credible only when tier distinctions stay explicit. Premium-class capabilities should not be implied for lower tiers.
items.notes: API Management guidance is credible only when tier distinctions stay explicit. Premium-class capabilities should not be implied for lower tiers.
items.guardrail: Do not generalize Premium classic multi-region behavior to all tiers.
recommendedActions: Record the exact tier under review before any export is generated.
Expose certificate, domain, and backend parity dependencies.
Use advisory export posture unless the tier posture is fully confirmed.

references.label: Enable Availability Zones for Azure API Management instances

detail.haSummary: API Management uses scale units with built-in redundancy. Premium classic supports availability zones and multi-region deployments, while several other tiers are single-datacenter or single-region only.
detail.drSummary: Regional DR is strongest in Premium classic through multi-region deployment with regional gateways. Other tiers have materially less DR capability and may need backup and restore or customer-managed duplicate deployments.
limitations: Premium classic is the only tier explicitly supporting multi-region deployments; not all tiers support zones
recommendedFor: Enterprise API platforms that can justify Premium classic for multi-region availability
notRecommendedFor: Consumption, Developer, or single-region tiers when the business requires strong native DR
testGuidance: Exercise region failure scenarios, long-running infrastructure changes, and certificate or domain rollout processes

items.title: Azure Cosmos DB topology posture
items.summary: Cosmos DB guidance is powerful but topology-sensitive. Review outputs must stay explicit about account shape, consistency, and region failover timing.
items.sourceName: Disaster recovery guidance for Azure Cosmos DB
items.notes: Cosmos DB guidance is powerful but topology-sensitive. Review outputs must stay explicit about account shape, consistency, and region failover timing.
items.guardrail: Do not treat one Cosmos DB topology as representative of the whole service.
recommendedActions: Capture account topology and consistency requirements before review.
Test SDK preferred-region behavior and region offline procedures.
Escalate restore-path timelines in leadership-ready material.

references.label: Disaster recovery guidance for Azure Cosmos DB

detail.haSummary: A single-region account with availability zones can survive a single-zone outage in-region.
detail.drSummary: Regional recovery behavior depends on account topology. Single-region accounts may need restore to another region if outage exceeds RTO. Multi-region accounts can reroute reads and, with multi-write or PPAF patterns, preserve higher availability.
limitations: Service-managed failover timing can take one hour or more; restore and online-region actions can take days
recommendedFor: Globally distributed apps that can use SDK-aware regional routing and well-planned account topology
notRecommendedFor: Single-region critical workloads with aggressive RTO and RPO targets
testGuidance: Test region outage procedures, SDK preferred-region behavior, and restore paths; avoid control-plane changes during outages

items.title: Azure Front Door ingress posture
items.summary: Front Door improves global ingress resiliency, but it is not the whole continuity strategy and can still require an alternate ingress path for mission-critical cases.
items.sourceName: Architecture best practices for Azure Front Door
items.notes: Front Door improves global ingress resiliency, but it is not the whole continuity strategy and can still require an alternate ingress path for mission-critical cases.
items.guardrail: Do not present Front Door alone as end-to-end DR.
recommendedActions: Check that multiple healthy origins exist before calling failover ready.
Record whether a secondary ingress path is required for the workload tier.
Validate origin health endpoints and failback procedures.

references.label: Architecture best practices for Azure Front Door
Global routing redundancy for mission-critical web applications

detail.haSummary: Azure Front Door is a globally distributed service designed to route traffic across multiple origins and regions. It supports active-active and active-passive origin strategies with health probes and edge routing.
detail.drSummary: Front Door itself is highly resilient, but Microsoft explicitly warns it can still be a potential single point of failure for mission-critical workloads. For higher DR assurance, add a secondary ingress path such as Traffic Manager directing to an alternate router.
limitations: Front Door is not the whole DR strategy; it depends on origin resilience and may need redundant routing only for mission-critical workloads
recommendedFor: Internet-facing apps deployed in multiple regions that need global routing and edge security
notRecommendedFor: Single-region backends with no redundant origin strategy or teams expecting Front Door alone to satisfy end-to-end DR
testGuidance: Test origin failover, health probe behavior, and any Traffic Manager manual failover runbook for alternate ingress

items.title: AKS platform posture
items.summary: AKS can support strong availability, but the platform only becomes decision ready when cluster design, dependent services, and operational runbooks are reviewed together.
items.notes: AKS can support strong availability, but the platform only becomes decision ready when cluster design, dependent services, and operational runbooks are reviewed together.
items.guardrail: Managed control planes do not remove the need for multi-region application design.
recommendedActions: Review node pool, zone, and dependency placement together.
Check ingress, state, secret, and registry dependencies before export.
Treat AKS guidance as advisory until operational drills are recorded.

detail.haSummary: AKS control plane is zone resilient by default in zone-supporting regions, but cluster availability still depends on node pool design, pod replicas, topology spread, autoscaling, and resilient dependencies.
detail.drSummary: AKS clusters are single-region resources. Regional DR requires separate clusters in another region, often coordinated with Fleet Manager or custom active-active or active-passive patterns, plus backup and restore for cluster resources and persistent volumes where needed.
limitations: AKS does not make a workload resilient by itself; single-cluster designs are not regional DR
recommendedFor: Container workloads that can invest in platform engineering and multi-cluster operations
notRecommendedFor: Teams expecting control-plane resiliency alone to make the whole workload highly available
testGuidance: Test zone failure with node drain or Chaos Studio and run multi-region failover drills plus restore exercises

items.title: Azure OpenAI regional posture
items.summary: Azure OpenAI continuity depends on duplicate regional deployments, quota parity, fallback routing, and client behavior. These are design-time controls, not UI claims.
items.sourceName: Business Continuity and Disaster Recovery (BCDR) considerations with Azure OpenAI in Azure AI Foundry Models
items.notes: Azure OpenAI continuity depends on duplicate regional deployments, quota parity, fallback routing, and client behavior. These are design-time controls, not UI claims.
items.guardrail: Do not imply regional continuity unless duplicate capacity and routing are both in place.
recommendedActions: Check quota parity in every target region before export.
Separate model deployment continuity from application continuity.
Require explicit override before including this posture in leadership packs.

references.label: Business Continuity and Disaster Recovery (BCDR) considerations with Azure OpenAI in Azure AI Foundry Models

detail.haSummary: Azure OpenAI resources are regional. For higher resiliency, Microsoft recommends Global Standard deployments when possible, with Data Zone as the next best option for geographic-boundary processing requirements.
detail.drSummary: If continuous availability matters, deploy at least two Azure OpenAI resources in different regions, duplicate model deployments, and place API Management or another AI gateway in front for load balancing, circuit breaking, and failover.
limitations: Model and quota parity are critical; source guidance is portal-version-specific and strongly prefers Global Standard when available
dependencies: dependent state stores such as Cosmos DB for agents
recommendedFor: AI applications that can deploy duplicate regional endpoints and manage gateway-based failover
notRecommendedFor: Teams assuming one Azure OpenAI resource is a complete DR strategy
testGuidance: Test regional endpoint failover, gateway policies, quota exhaustion behavior, and dependency recovery for agent state

items.title: Azure Service Bus durability posture
items.summary: Service Bus can support strong continuity patterns, but message durability and promotion semantics remain easy to misstate without tier-aware review.
items.notes: Service Bus can support strong continuity patterns, but message durability and promotion semantics remain easy to misstate without tier-aware review.
items.guardrail: Metadata Geo-DR is not message-data protection.
recommendedActions: Confirm whether queued message durability is a hard requirement or an acceptable trade-off.
Use Premium tier when cross-region continuity must stay explicit.
Review RBAC, networking, and alias behavior as part of every export.

detail.haSummary: Service Bus provides built-in zone resilience behavior and automatically reroutes to healthy replicas during zone issues.
detail.drSummary: For region outages, Premium tier supports Geo-Replication for metadata plus data and metadata Geo-Disaster Recovery for metadata only. Microsoft recommends Geo-Replication for most DR scenarios.
limitations: Standard and Basic lack native multi-region features; Service Bus itself has no backup and restore for message data
recommendedFor: Critical messaging systems needing managed broker semantics and clear regional recovery options
notRecommendedFor: Workloads that assume metadata Geo-DR preserves queued messages
testGuidance: Run planned promotion tests and validate message durability expectations before calling a design enterprise ready

items.title: Azure Virtual Machines infrastructure posture
items.summary: VM continuity stays architecture-dependent. Azure features help, but traffic rerouting, data replication, and failover runbooks remain customer-owned.
items.notes: VM continuity stays architecture-dependent. Azure features help, but traffic rerouting, data replication, and failover runbooks remain customer-owned.
items.guardrail: A single zonal VM is not a disaster recovery design.
recommendedActions: Validate both traffic rerouting and data replication paths.
Treat Site Recovery as one control in a wider regional pattern.
Require drill evidence before using VM patterns in decision packs.

detail.haSummary: Azure VMs can use availability zones, but VM-level zone resiliency is customer designed rather than fully managed. Zone failure handling requires traffic rerouting and operational response by the customer.
detail.drSummary: VMs are single-region resources. For regional DR, deploy to another region and use Azure Site Recovery or a custom multi-region pattern with replication and failover.
limitations: Single zonal VM remains down until zone recovery; Site Recovery zone-to-zone has limitations; customer owns rerouting
recommendedFor: Stateful workloads that need infrastructure control and can absorb operational complexity
notRecommendedFor: Teams expecting PaaS-style automatic zone failover without runbooks
testGuidance: Test with Chaos Studio for zone loss and exercise Site Recovery failover and failback regularly

items.title: Azure Virtual WAN topology posture
items.summary: Virtual WAN provides strong hub-level resilience, but regional DR starts only when the network is designed and tested across multiple hubs and regions.
items.sourceName: Architecture best practices for Azure Virtual WAN
items.notes: Virtual WAN provides strong hub-level resilience, but regional DR starts only when the network is designed and tested across multiple hubs and regions.
items.guardrail: A single hub is not regional DR.
recommendedActions: Capture hub placement and route intent before concluding maturity.
Test path selection under degraded conditions, not only normal failover.
Escalate routing asymmetry and capacity risks into leadership summaries.

references.label: Architecture best practices for Azure Virtual WAN
Virtual WAN network topology in an Azure landing zone

detail.haSummary: Virtual WAN provides resiliency inside a hub through active-active gateways and, in the Standard SKU, zone-redundant gateway deployment where supported. It also supports layered redundancy across zones, connections, and routes.
detail.drSummary: Regional DR requires deploying multiple hubs across regions and planning routing for automatic or manual failover. A single hub is not regional DR.
limitations: Gateways do not auto-scale instantly; routing asymmetry and capacity planning matter during failover
recommendedFor: Enterprise transit networks that need scalable branch, user, and VNet connectivity with resilience layers
notRecommendedFor: Single-hub architectures presented as regional DR
testGuidance: Run multihub failover drills, test branch and user path selection, and validate routing under degraded conditions

items.title: Azure VPN Gateway network posture
items.summary: VPN Gateway can improve Azure-side resilience, but enterprise continuity still depends on on-premises device diversity, tunnel design, and routing validation.
items.sourceName: Reliability in Azure virtual network gateways
items.notes: VPN Gateway can improve Azure-side resilience, but enterprise continuity still depends on on-premises device diversity, tunnel design, and routing validation.
items.guardrail: A managed gateway does not remove the on-premises single point of failure.
recommendedActions: Check remote device support for active-active or equivalent redundancy.
Require routing drill evidence from both Azure and on-premises perspectives.
Keep hybrid dependencies visible in every architecture export.

references.label: Reliability in Azure virtual network gateways

detail.haSummary: Azure VPN Gateway uses two gateway VMs and provides active-standby by default, with zone redundancy available on supported SKUs and active-active mode for higher availability.
detail.drSummary: Regional DR is customer-managed and generally requires another gateway in another region plus resilient on-premises devices and routing. The gateway service alone does not provide full end-to-end DR.
limitations: Overall reliability depends on both Azure gateway and on-premises side; point-to-site reconnect behavior differs from site-to-site
recommendedFor: Hybrid networks that need resilient site-to-site or VNet-to-VNet connectivity
notRecommendedFor: Architectures that ignore on-premises device resilience or rely on a single tunnel or device
testGuidance: Test gateway failover, tunnel loss, and regional rerouting from both Azure and on-premises perspectives

notRecommendedFor: Teams assuming a single function app has built-in regional DR

items.title: Dynamics 365 continuity posture
items.summary: D365 continuity statements are usually tenant and workload specific. Review guidance should stay visible for discovery but not act as a default enterprise baseline.
items.sourceName: Business continuity and disaster recovery for Dynamics 365 SaaS apps
items.notes: D365 continuity statements are usually tenant and workload specific. Review guidance should stay visible for discovery but not act as a default enterprise baseline.
items.guardrail: Do not generalize business application recovery claims across all D365 implementations.
recommendedActions: Validate product-specific continuity commitments with the service owner.
Capture tenant customizations that affect failover or recovery behavior.
Exclude from default export until the workload scope is explicit.

references.label: Business continuity and disaster recovery for Dynamics 365 SaaS apps

detail.haSummary: Dynamics 365 production environments include built-in in-region resilience across at least two availability zones with near-zero RPO and less than five-minute RTO in most zone-failure cases per Microsoft.
detail.drSummary: Cross-region DR is app-dependent. Some environments support self-service DR and drills, but support is not universal and several capabilities or products have limitations or are not supported.
limitations: Backups are not cross-region protection unless self-service DR is enabled; some apps remain unsupported or preview-only
recommendedFor: Critical production Dynamics environments that use supported apps and managed environment prerequisites
notRecommendedFor: Organizations assuming all Dynamics 365 products have uniform self-service DR support
testGuidance: Run DR drills in supported environments and document external integration recovery steps before an actual outage

items.title: Microsoft Fabric experience posture
items.summary: Fabric guidance stays experience-specific. OneLake-backed paths can be strong, but broad platform statements remain risky without workload context.
items.notes: Fabric guidance stays experience-specific. OneLake-backed paths can be strong, but broad platform statements remain risky without workload context.
items.guardrail: Do not export Fabric guidance as GA-ready unless the experience and data path are explicitly confirmed.
recommendedActions: Capture the exact Fabric experience under review.
Validate read-only and recovery behavior for the chosen workload.
Keep Fabric isolated from default exports unless manually included.

detail.haSummary: Fabric makes commercially reasonable efforts to support availability zones for supported experiences, but support is partial and experience-specific. OneLake-backed experiences inherit stronger resiliency than external data sources.
detail.drSummary: Fabric cross-region recovery depends on the Fabric DR feature set and customer preparation. OneLake cross-region replication can be enabled, but many experiences fall back to read-only access or require customer actions after failover.
limitations: Availability zone support is partial; many write paths and some experience types have failover limitations
recommendedFor: Fabric workloads that can tolerate experience-specific limitations during regional failover and can standardize on OneLake
notRecommendedFor: Workloads assuming every Fabric experience has identical HA and DR guarantees
testGuidance: Test DR toggles, read-only behavior, gateway dependencies, and experience-specific recovery steps before relying on Fabric for strict DR targets
